steps.title: Step 1
steps.content: In right triangle XYZ, angle X has an opposite leg and the hypotenuse. By definition, $\sin X = \dfrac{\text{opposite}}{\text{hypotenuse}}$.

steps.title: Step 2
steps.content: Angles X and Y are complementary (sum to 90°). The side opposite X is the side adjacent to Y. So $\cos Y = \dfrac{\text{adjacent to }Y}{\text{hypotenuse}} = \dfrac{\text{opposite to }X}{\text{hypotenuse}}$.

steps.title: Step 3
steps.content: Therefore $\sin X = \cos Y$ exactly — they share the same numerator (the leg between the right angle and angle Y) and the same denominator (the hypotenuse).

steps.title: Step 4
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘$\tan X$’: $\tan X = \text{opposite}/\text{adjacent}$, a different ratio that does not equal $\sin X$ in general. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘$\tan Y$’: $\tan Y = \text{opposite to }Y / \text{adjacent to }Y$, which uses a completely different pair of sides. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘$\cos X$’: $\cos X = \text{adjacent to }X / \text{hypotenuse}$, which uses the adjacent side to X, not the opposite side. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘$\cos Y$’: $\cos Y = \text{adjacent to }Y / \text{hypotenuse} = \text{opposite to }X / \text{hypotenuse} = \sin X$. ✓
That confirms D is correct.
